metadata.design_note: El diccionario acumulado persiste a través de TODAS las iteraciones en orden (k=2 completo → k=3 completo → ...). Cada combinación refina la hipótesis vigente. El orden de procesamiento es lexicográfico.

best_step.accumulated_dict:
ŋa = I (object)
ŋabə = I (subject variant)
nɤ = you (sg) (subject or object)
nɤbə = you (sg) (subject variant)
nuʔrum = you (pl) (subject or object)
nirum = we (subject or object)
tarum = they (subject)
ati = third‑person singular pronoun (subject or object)
kəmə = object‑linker (optional; placed after subject before object or before fronted object)
ka = present‑tense marker (pre‑verb) or copular “be”
kan = present‑tense marker (post‑verb, especially in questions)
ran, tɤʔ, tʰi, tuʔ, tiʔ, tʰu, tʰɤ = past‑tense markers (post‑verb)
kɤ = go (verb)
ʒip = sleep (verb)
lapkʰi = see (verb)
lan = beat (verb)
cʰam = know (verb)
ki, ku, ri, rɤ = clause‑final nuance particles (optional, appear before ne)
ne = sentence‑final question particle
best_step.accumulated_rules:
1. A clause normally begins with a subject pronoun (or its –bə variant) or a noun/pronoun functioning as subject.
2. Basic word order is **Subject – Object – Verb**. When the object follows the subject directly, no linker is required.
3. The optional linker **kəmə** can appear **after the subject and before the object** (S kəmə O V) or when the object is fronted (O kəmə S V). Its use is optional even in S O V clauses, but it is common in interrogatives.
4. **ka** placed before a verb marks present tense; it can also serve as the copular “be”.
5. Past tense is marked by a **post‑verb** morpheme selected from {ran, tɤʔ, tʰi, tuʔ, tiʔ, tʰu, tʰɤ}. (All function equivalently as past markers.)
6. Present tense can also be marked **post‑verb** with **kan**, especially in interrogative clauses.
7. In the absence of an explicit tense marker, the default interpretation is present.
8. Interrogative clauses end with the particle **ne**. One of the clause‑final nuance particles (**ki, ku, ri, rɤ**) may appear immediately before **ne**; their presence is optional.
9. When **ka** functions as the copular “be”, a following past marker (e.g., **tiʔ**) yields a past‑tense copular clause (“was/were”).

hypotheses: ati kəmə nirum lapkʰi tʰi ne
tarum kəmə nuʔrum cʰam ran ne
nirum kəmə tarum lan ki ne
nirum kəmə nɤ cʰam tiʔ ne

steps.accumulated_rules:
1. A clause normally begins with a subject pronoun (or its –bə variant) or a noun/pronoun functioning as subject; the object may be fronted before the subject.
2. Present tense can be marked by **ka** before the verb or **kan** after the verb.
3. Past tense can be marked by pre‑verb markers (tuʔ, tʰi) or post‑verb markers (tɤʔ, tiʔ, tʰu, ran, tʰɤ). The marker **tʰi** may appear either before or after the verb; **tʰɤ** appears after the verb.
4. The verb follows any pre‑verb tense marker; a post‑verb tense marker follows the verb.
5. An object pronoun may appear before the subject (fronting) or after the subject; when present, it is linked to the subject by **kəmə**.
6. **kəmə** is a linker particle that connects the two core arguments (subject and object) regardless of their order.
7. **ki** can appear after the verb; its precise function is still undetermined.
8. **rɤ** can appear after the verb before the question particle; its function is still undetermined.
9. **ri**, **ku**, and **ri** can appear after the verb (and before **ne**) in questions; their functions are still unknown.
10. The question particle **ne** terminates a clause that is a question.

steps.accumulated_rules:
1. A clause normally begins with a subject pronoun (or its –bə variant) or a noun/pronoun functioning as subject.
2. The object may be placed before the subject; when it precedes the subject it is linked to the subject by **kəmə**. If the object follows the subject, **kəmə** is optional and often omitted.
3. Tense can be marked by a pre‑verb marker (e.g., **tuʔ**, **tʰi**) or a post‑verb marker (e.g., **tɤʔ**, **tiʔ**, **tʰu**, **ran**, **tʰɤ**). The same marker may appear either before or after the verb.
4. The verb follows any pre‑verb tense marker; a post‑verb tense marker follows the verb.
5. Present tense is commonly marked by **ka** before the verb or **kan** after the verb; **ka** can also function as a copular/auxiliary verb that may combine with a past marker.
6. In interrogative clauses the final particle **ne** terminates the sentence. Additional clause‑final particles (**ku**, **ki**, **ri**, **rɤ**) may appear after the verb (and before **ne**) and seem to serve discourse‑ or question‑type functions; their exact meanings are still undetermined.

5. The verb follows any pre‑verb tense marker; a post‑verb tense marker follows the verb.
6. In the absence of an explicit tense marker, the default interpretation is present.

8. Interrogative clauses end with the particle **ne**. One of the clause‑final particles (**ki**, **ku**, **ri**, **rɤ**) may appear immediately before **ne** to mark a question or add discourse nuance.
9. The same pronoun form can serve as both subject and object (e.g., **ŋa** = I / me; **ati** = he/she / him/her).

2. Basic word order is **Subject – Object – Verb**. When the object follows the subject directly, no linker is required.
3. The optional linker **kəmə** can appear **after the subject and before the object** (S kəmə O V) or when the object is fronted (O kəmə S V). Its use is optional even in S O V clauses.
4. **ka** placed before a verb marks present tense; it can also serve as the copular “be”.
5. Past tense is marked by a **post‑verb** morpheme selected from {ran, tɤʔ, tʰi, tuʔ, tiʔ, tʰu, tʰɤ}. (All function equivalently as past markers.)
6. Present tense can also be marked **post‑verb** with **kan**, especially in interrogative clauses.
7. In the absence of an explicit tense marker, the default interpretation is present.
8. Interrogative clauses end with the particle **ne**. One of the clause‑final nuance particles (**ki, ku, ri, rɤ**) may appear immediately before **ne**; their presence is optional.
9. When **ka** functions as the copular “be”, a following past marker (e.g., **tiʔ**) yields a past‑tense copular clause (“was/were”).

2. Basic word order is **Subject – Object – Verb**. When the object follows the subject directly, no linker is required, but the optional linker **kəmə** may be inserted, especially in interrogative clauses.
3. **kəmə** can also appear when the object is fronted (Object kəmə Subject Verb) or even when the object follows the subject; its use is optional.
4. **ka** placed before a verb marks present tense; it can also serve as the copular “be”.
5. Past tense is marked by a **post‑verb** morpheme selected from {ran, tɤʔ, tʰi, tuʔ, tiʔ, tʰu, tʰɤ}. All function equivalently as past markers.
6. Present tense can also be marked **post‑verb** with **kan**, especially in interrogative clauses.
7. In the absence of an explicit tense marker, the default interpretation is present.
8. Interrogative clauses end with the particle **ne**. One of the clause‑final nuance particles (**ki, ku, ri, rɤ**) may appear immediately before **ne**; their presence is optional and does not alter core meaning.
9. When **ka** functions as the copular “be”, a following past marker (e.g., **tiʔ**) yields a past‑tense copular clause (“was/were”).

steps.accumulated_dict:

ŋabə = I (subject variant)
nɤ = you (sg) (object or default pronoun)

nirum = we (subject or object)

kəmə = object‑linker (optional; used before object or in questions)
ka = present‑tense marker (pre‑verb) or copular “be”
kan = present‑tense marker (post‑verb, especially in questions)
ran = past‑tense marker (post‑verb)
tɤʔ = past‑tense marker (post‑verb)
tʰi = past‑tense marker (post‑verb)
tuʔ = past‑tense marker (post‑verb)
tiʔ = past‑tense marker (post‑verb)
tʰu = past‑tense marker (post‑verb)
tʰɤ = past‑tense marker (post‑verb)
kɤ = go (verb)
ʒip = sleep (verb)
lapkʰi = see (verb)
lan = beat (verb)
cʰam = know (verb)
ki = clause‑final nuance particle (optional)
ku = clause‑final nuance particle (optional)
ri = clause‑final nuance particle (optional)
rɤ = clause‑final nuance particle (optional)
ne = sentence‑final question particle
